title: Chapter 16: The Last Pier
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D12+D7 -->
The last consultation on the Santa Monica Pier was at sunset.

Chloe didn't know it would be the last one until it was happening — the way you don't know something is an ending until you're inside it and suddenly realize the shape of it. The woman who sat down was about sixty, wearing a REI vest and hiking boots, with the kind of face that had been through things and come out the other side.
"Hi," the woman said. "My name is Diane. I'm not here for a consultation."
"Then why are you here?"
"I wanted to thank you."
Chloe tilted her head. "For what?"
"My daughter is Jess. The strawberry girl."
Chloe went very still.

"She called me," Diane said. "For the first time in eight months. She told me things — things I didn't know. Things she'd been carrying alone. We talked for three hours. And then she came home. Actually home. Not just a visit. She moved back for a while. She's in the guest room right now, painting. She used to paint in high school and then she stopped. She started again."
Diane's eyes were wet but her voice was steady — the same steady that Jess had had in her video.

"I don't know what you said to her at that strawberry farm," Diane continued. "She tried to explain it. Something about strawberries and validation and calling your mother. I don't fully understand it. But whatever it was, it brought my daughter back. So I drove here from Bakersfield to tell you: thank you."
Chloe didn't say anything for a long time.
Sam, on the adjacent bench, had stopped typing. She was watching — not the spreadsheet, for once, but the two women on the bench.
"I didn't do anything," Chloe said finally. "I just told her to call you."

"That's the thing," Diane said. "Everyone else told her to meditate, to journal, to optimize her morning routine, to buy a supplement subscription. You told her to call her mother. No one else said that."
Chloe looked at the ocean.
"The simplest things are the hardest to remember," she said. "That's why I wear a crown."
Diane looked at the cracked plastic crown. "Does it work?"
"Most of the time."
"Most of the time is more than most people get."
Diane stood up and pulled a small object from her vest pocket — a painted rock, smooth and flat, with a tiny strawberry on it. "Jess made this. She wanted you to have it."
Chloe took it. The strawberry was lopsided — the work of someone who hadn't painted in years but was starting again.
"Tell her the kingdom received her tribute," Chloe said, "and that the strawberry is perfect exactly as it is."
Diane smiled for the first time. "I will."
After Diane left, Sam closed her laptop. "Column P," she said. "Things We've Actually Changed. Entry: a mother has her daughter back."
Chloe was still holding the rock. "That's better than any candle."
"That's better than any spreadsheet."
Chloe turned to her. "Nothing is better than the spreadsheet. The spreadsheet is sacred."
Sam almost smiled. "Okay. Equal to the spreadsheet."
"Acceptable."
